frontmatter.title: Negation invariance of the square root relation

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: By relational extensionality, it suffices to check that for any x \in \mathbb {R}_{ \geq 0} and y \in \mathbb {R}, we have x \mathrel { { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {neg} \circ \mathsf {sq} \right ) \mathclose {}} } y if and only if x \mathrel { \mathsf {sq}}y. We compute:
block.tex: \begin {aligned} x \mathrel { { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {neg} \circ \mathsf {sq} \right ) \mathclose {}} } y & \Longleftrightarrow \exists z \in \mathbb {R} \mathpunct {.} x \mathrel { \mathsf {sq}}z \land z \mathrel { \mathsf {neg}}y \\ & \Longleftrightarrow \exists z \in \mathbb {R} \mathpunct {.} x=z^2 \land z =-y \\ & \Longleftrightarrow x= { \mathopen {} \left ( -y \right ) \mathclose {}} ^2 \\ & \Longleftrightarrow x = y^2 \\ & \Longleftrightarrow x \mathrel { \mathsf {sq}}y \end {aligned}

frontmatter.title: Associativity and unit laws of relational composition

mainmatter.p: Relational composition is associative and has the identity relation as a neutral element.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Using the above, we can compute the full composites:

For the right and left neutrality, we must prove that R \circ \mathsf {id}_{ A } = R = \mathsf {id}_{ B } \circ R for all r \colon A \mathbin { \nrightarrow } B. We prove only the first law, as the other proof is analogous:

mainmatter.p: A (binary) relation R from a set A to a set B, written R \colon A \mathbin { \nrightarrow } B or R \in \mathrm {Rel} { \mathopen {} \left ( A,B \right ) \mathclose {}} is defined to be a subset R \subseteq A \times B. We shall typically write a \mathrel {R}b for { \mathopen {} \left ( a,b \right ) \mathclose {}} \in R. More generally, a relation between multiple sets { \mathopen {} \left ( A_i \right ) \mathclose {}} _{i \in I} is defined to be a subset of the cartesian product \prod _{i \in I} A_i.

Under the correspondence between boolean matrices and finite relations (, ), matrix products of boolean matrices correspond to relational composites. In particular, given M \in \mathrm {Mat}_{ \mathbb {B} } { \mathopen {} \left ( l , m \right ) \mathclose {}} and N \in \mathrm {Mat}_{ \mathbb {B} } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , n \right ) \mathclose {}}, we have:

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We use the fact that the additive operation of the boolean semiring is disjunction and the multiplicative operation is conjunction:

frontmatter.title: Associativity and unit laws of relational composition

mainmatter.p: Relational composition is associative and has the identity relation as a neutral element.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Using the above, we can compute the full composites:

For the right and left neutrality, we must prove that R \circ \mathsf {id}_{ A } = R = \mathsf {id}_{ B } \circ R for all r \colon A \mathbin { \nrightarrow } B. We prove only the first law, as the other proof is analogous:

frontmatter.title: Negation invariance of the square root relation

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: By relational extensionality, it suffices to check that for any x \in \mathbb {R}_{ \geq 0} and y \in \mathbb {R}, we have x \mathrel { { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {neg} \circ \mathsf {sq} \right ) \mathclose {}} } y if and only if x \mathrel { \mathsf {sq}}y. We compute:
block.tex: \begin {aligned} x \mathrel { { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {neg} \circ \mathsf {sq} \right ) \mathclose {}} } y & \Longleftrightarrow \exists z \in \mathbb {R} \mathpunct {.} x \mathrel { \mathsf {sq}}z \land z \mathrel { \mathsf {neg}}y \\ & \Longleftrightarrow \exists z \in \mathbb {R} \mathpunct {.} x=z^2 \land z =-y \\ & \Longleftrightarrow x= { \mathopen {} \left ( -y \right ) \mathclose {}} ^2 \\ & \Longleftrightarrow x = y^2 \\ & \Longleftrightarrow x \mathrel { \mathsf {sq}}y \end {aligned}

mainmatter.p: Relations between finite sets can be desribed in a more computationally friendly way by their tabulation as matrices. In particular, we shall see in that an { \mathopen {} \left ( m \times n \right ) \mathclose {}}-matrix over the boolean semiring is precisely the same thing as a relation from { \mathopen {} \left [ m \right ] \mathclose {}} to { \mathopen {} \left [ n \right ] \mathclose {}}, where { \mathopen {} \left [ l \right ] \mathclose {}} = { \mathopen {} \left \{ i \mid 0 \leq i < l \right \} \mathclose {}} is the set of natural numbers strictly smaller than l. Then we will see that relational composition is, under this correspondence, the same as matrix multiplication.

For natural numbers m and n, an { \mathopen {} \left ( m \times n \right ) \mathclose {}}-matrix over a semiring { \mathopen {} \left ( S,0, \oplus ,1, \odot \right ) \mathclose {}} is given by entries M_{i,j} \in S for all i \in { \mathopen {} \left [ m \right ] \mathclose {}} and j \in { \mathopen {} \left [ n \right ] \mathclose {}}. We will write \mathrm {Mat}_{ S } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , m \right ) \mathclose {}} for the set of { \mathopen {} \left ( m \times n \right ) \mathclose {}}-matrices.

{ \mathopen {} \left ( m \times n \right ) \mathclose {}}-matrices can be depicted in tables or grids with rows in the first dimension and columns in the second dimension. For example, let M \in \mathrm {Mat}_{ \mathbb {B} } { \mathopen {} \left ( 3 , 2 \right ) \mathclose {}} be the matrix over the booleans defined by the following equation:
Then M is depicted by the following table with three rows and two columns:
For any m \in \mathbb {N}, we define the identity { \mathopen {} \left ( m \times m \right ) \mathclose {}}-matrix over a given semiring S as follows:
The identity matrix is sometimes called the diagonal matrix, for reasons that become apparent when visualising it according to :

frontmatter.title: Correspondence between matrices and finite relations
The matrix associated to a finite relation

mainmatter.p: Let R \colon { \mathopen {} \left [ m \right ] \mathclose {}} \mathbin { \nrightarrow } { \mathopen {} \left [ n \right ] \mathclose {}} be a relation; for any semiring S, we may form the { \mathopen {} \left ( n \times n \right ) \mathclose {}}-matrix over S associated to R as follows:

We have defined a function \operatorname { \underline {mat}} _S \colon \mathrm {Rel} { \mathopen {} \left ( { \mathopen {} \left [ m \right ] \mathclose {}} , { \mathopen {} \left [ n \right ] \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Mat}_{ S } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , n \right ) \mathclose {}}.

frontmatter.title: The relation associated to a matrix

mainmatter.p: Let M be an { \mathopen {} \left ( n \times n \right ) \mathclose {}}-matrix over a semiring S. We define the relation associated to M below:

We have defined a function \operatorname { \underline {rel}} _S \colon \mathrm {Mat}_{ S } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , n \right ) \mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Rel} { \mathopen {} \left ( { \mathopen {} \left [ m \right ] \mathclose {}} , { \mathopen {} \left [ n \right ] \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}}.

frontmatter.title: A retraction from matrices to finite relations

mainmatter.p: The associated matrix function \operatorname { \underline {mat}} _S \colon \mathrm {Rel} { \mathopen {} \left ( { \mathopen {} \left [ m \right ] \mathclose {}} , { \mathopen {} \left [ n \right ] \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Mat}_{ S } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , n \right ) \mathclose {}} is a section of the associated relation function \operatorname { \underline {rel}} _S \colon \mathrm {Mat}_{ S } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , n \right ) \mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Rel} { \mathopen {} \left ( { \mathopen {} \left [ m \right ] \mathclose {}} , { \mathopen {} \left [ n \right ] \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} for any semiring S.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We must check that \operatorname { \underline {rel}} _S \circ \operatorname { \underline {mat}} _S = \mathsf {id}_{ \mathrm {Rel} { \mathopen {} \left ( { \mathopen {} \left [ m \right ] \mathclose {}} , { \mathopen {} \left [ n \right ] \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} }. Fixing a relation R \colon { \mathopen {} \left [ m \right ] \mathclose {}} \mathbin { \nrightarrow } { \mathopen {} \left [ n \right ] \mathclose {}}, we compute:

mainmatter.p: The other composite \operatorname { \underline {mat}} _S \circ \operatorname { \underline {rel}} _S \colon \mathrm {Mat}_{ S } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , n \right ) \mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Mat}_{ S } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , n \right ) \mathclose {}} is not in general the identity function, but is (necessarily) an idempotent on the set of matrices over S. We will see that this idempotent, in some sense, measures the degree to which the base semiring S is not boolean.

frontmatter.title: Finite relations as matrices over the booleans

mainmatter.p: The idempotent { \operatorname { \underline {mat}} _ \mathbb {B} \circ \operatorname { \underline {rel}} _ \mathbb {B} \colon \mathrm {Mat}_{ \mathbb {B} } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , n \right ) \mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Mat}_{ \mathbb {B} } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , n \right ) \mathclose {}} } is in fact the identity function on matrices over the boolean semiring \mathbb {B}.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We fix a matrix M \in \mathrm {Mat}_{ \mathbb {B} } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , n \right ) \mathclose {}} and compute:
Because \mathbb {B} is the boolean semiring, any scalar s \in S is either 0 or 1. Therefore, we conclude:

mainmatter.p: Thus we conclude that an { \mathopen {} \left ( m \times n \right ) \mathclose {}}-matrix over the booleans is the same thing as a relation from { \mathopen {} \left [ m \right ] \mathclose {}} to { \mathopen {} \left [ n \right ] \mathclose {}}.

Although we do not explore it in this course, the viewpoint of matrix multiplication as relational composition generalises to a correct explanation of the role of matrices in geometry as presentations of linear maps between vector spaces in terms of an (uncanonical) choice of basis.

Let M be an { \mathopen {} \left ( l \times m \right ) \mathclose {}}-matrix and let N be a { \mathopen {} \left ( m \times n \right ) \mathclose {}}-matrix. The product of M and N, written M \cdot N, is the following { \mathopen {} \left ( l \times n \right ) \mathclose {}}-matrix:
mainmatter.tex: { \mathopen {} \left ( N \cdot M \right ) \mathclose {}} _{i \in { \mathopen {} \left [ l \right ] \mathclose {}} ,j \in { \mathopen {} \left [ n \right ] \mathclose {}} } = \bigoplus _{k \in { \mathopen {} \left [ m \right ] \mathclose {}} } M_{i,k} \cdot N_{k,j}

frontmatter.title: Associativity and unit laws of matrix products

mainmatter.p: The product of matrices is associative and has the identity matrix as neutral element.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: For associativity, fix matrices L \in \mathrm {Mat}_{ S } { \mathopen {} \left ( k , l \right ) \mathclose {}}, M \in \mathrm {Mat}_{ S } { \mathopen {} \left ( l , m \right ) \mathclose {}} and N \in \mathrm {Mat}_{ S } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , n \right ) \mathclose {}} to check that { \mathopen {} \left ( N \cdot M \right ) \mathclose {}} \cdot L = N \cdot { \mathopen {} \left ( M \cdot L \right ) \mathclose {}}. Below we use the associativity of multiplication, commutativity of addition, and distributivity of multiplication over addition in the semiring S:
For one unit law, we fix M \in \mathrm {Mat}_{ S } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , n \right ) \mathclose {}} and recall the definition of the identity matrix to check that M \cdot I^m = M.

Unfolding the definition of I^m_{i,j} as given in , we see that the iterated sum above expands to m-1 copies of 0 \cdot M_{k,j} for various k \not = i and one copy of 1 \cdot M_{i,j}. Thus, we conclude that M \cdot I^m_{i,j} = M_{i,j} using the absorption and unit laws for multiplication as well as the unit laws for addition in S.
The other unit law follows in an analogous way.

mainmatter.p: Under the correspondence between boolean matrices and finite relations (, ), matrix products of boolean matrices correspond to relational composites. In particular, given M \in \mathrm {Mat}_{ \mathbb {B} } { \mathopen {} \left ( l , m \right ) \mathclose {}} and N \in \mathrm {Mat}_{ \mathbb {B} } { \mathopen {} \left ( m , n \right ) \mathclose {}}, we have:

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We use the fact that the additive operation of the boolean semiring is disjunction and the multiplicative operation is conjunction:
